lens.included_activities: No operating establishment in the defined NAICS population qualifies. The fixed lens would require a privately transferable U.S. lower-middle-market firm providing recurring outsourced education-program administration to external customers, while NAICS 923110 is expressly limited to government establishments.
lens.excluded_activities: Federal, state, and local education departments and agencies; school districts and instructional institutions; private education consultants, software vendors, grant administrators classified elsewhere; captive units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Establishments administer public education funds, policy, intergovernmental activity, statistics, data collection, grants, monitoring, and centralized programs under statutory authority and public appropriations, not commercial recurring-service revenue from external customers.

a.bridge: Census and Department of Education sources describe the functions rather than measuring wage-weighted task time. I mapped drafting, review, grant administration, data collection and analysis, reporting, technical assistance, scheduling, and knowledge retrieval to exposure, while leaving policy authority, supervision, negotiation, investigations, stakeholder judgment, and accountable decisions unexposed. The mapping may overstate exposure where nominally administrative work requires legal judgment or sensitive case handling and understate it where agencies retain highly manual document workflows.
a.rationale: The industry's defined work is unusually information intensive, so language, retrieval, classification, analytics, and workflow tools can substitute for portions of current administrative effort or avoid added hiring. Human authority and review remain central because the work allocates funds, monitors compliance, interprets policy, and affects public rights and educational access.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-use study across federal and state education agencies that codes current hours by task and identifies which workflows are already automated, supplemented by production telemetry from grant, monitoring, reporting, and public-service systems.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was found for this government-only industry.
The estimate concerns current not-yet-automated tasks, but agencies differ substantially in legacy-system maturity.
Exposure does not imply authority to automate final eligibility, funding, or enforcement decisions.

rho.rationale: Federal evidence shows rapid growth in reported AI use cases and material potential for internal operations and service delivery, supporting meaningful implementation. Five-year conversion is limited by procurement cycles, legacy integration, workforce rules, privacy, security, records retention, accessibility, data quality, policy compliance, and mandatory human accountability for consequential uses.
rho.change_evidence: Education-agency AI inventories and audited before-and-after results for grant review, monitoring, reporting, technical assistance, correspondence, and data-quality workflows, including cycle time, paid hours, error rates, appeals, and privacy incidents.
caveats: GAO's adoption evidence covers selected federal agencies, not state and local education agencies specifically.
State procurement, civil-service, records, and student-data rules can differ from federal requirements.
Implementation may augment staff or absorb backlogs rather than reduce payroll.

e.rationale: The authoritative 2022 NAICS definition states that this industry comprises government establishments. Government agencies are not privately transferable LMM firms supplying an outsourced service to external customers, so the eligible share under the fixed lens is zero by construction.
e.change_evidence: A formal Census NAICS revision that admits private operating firms to 923110, or record-level classification evidence showing private commercial establishments are included despite the published definition.
caveats: Private contractors may perform related work but are classified in other industries and are outside this record.
The injected LMM firm-count input is missing because government establishments are outside the relevant business datasets; it must not be replaced with a private-contractor estimate.

s5.rationale: A qualifying five-year control-transfer probability is not defined because the industry contains government agencies and the fixed lens has no eligible firms. Elections, appointments, reorganizations, or transfers of statutory responsibility are not transfers of a privately owned operating company.
s5.change_evidence: This primitive would become estimable only if authoritative classification evidence established a nonzero population of privately controlled LMM firms inside NAICS 923110, followed by verified control-transfer histories for that population.
caveats: Zero was not imputed because there is no eligible-firm population on which to define a transfer probability.
Government restructurings and outsourcing contracts are not qualifying control transfers.

q.rationale: Commercial retention is not defined for this population. Education agencies operate through public appropriations and grant authority rather than customer contracts whose renewal pricing or competitive pass-through would determine retained operator benefit.
q.change_evidence: This primitive would require a valid in-industry population of private operators plus customer contracts, renewal pricing, procurement terms, and post-implementation margin data; related contractors classified outside 923110 cannot supply that evidence for this record.
caveats: Budget savings may be redeployed, returned, or reduce future appropriations, but that is not commercial benefit retention by a transferable operator.
Grant recipients and vendors are counterparties to agencies, not the lens operators in this NAICS code.

d5.bridge: NCES projects total public and private elementary and secondary enrollment to fall another 6 percent from 2020 to 2030. Converting that broader ten-year beneficiary-volume trend to a five-year horizon implies a modest decline, but education-program administration is less enrollment-elastic because formula and discretionary grants, policy, monitoring, reporting, and compliance persist at federal and state levels. The proxy likely understates demand if new programs or reporting mandates expand and overstates it if consolidation or deregulation removes administrative work.
d5.rationale: Student enrollment is a useful demand anchor but not a direct workload measure. Statutory grants, intergovernmental coordination, data collection, compliance monitoring, and public accountability create durable administrative quantity even with modest enrollment decline, while policy-driven consolidation or reduced reporting can lower the current service basket.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year federal and state education-agency workload forecasts covering grant counts and dollars, monitoring cases, reporting collections, regulatory requirements, beneficiary contacts, appropriated staffing, and enacted program consolidations.
caveats: Enrollment is not a direct measure of administration output and the NCES projection vintage includes pandemic-era changes.
Federal, state, and local workloads may move in opposite directions when authority is devolved or programs are consolidated.
The range describes constant-quality administrative demand, not appropriations or employment.

o.rationale: Nearly all surviving demand still requires an accountable public agency because allocating funds, issuing policy, monitoring compliance, collecting official statistics, and coordinating governments are exercises of statutory authority. Software and contractors can perform components, but they do not replace the responsible governmental operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Enacted statutes, binding reorganization plans, or operating evidence showing education-program administration transferred entirely from public agencies to self-service systems or non-government operators, with the share of workload affected.
caveats: Agency consolidation can change which public operator performs the work without eliminating the operator requirement.
Outsourcing support tasks does not remove the government's accountable decision authority.

narrative.executive_view: AI can materially assist the document, data, grant, reporting, and service workflows of education-program administration, but this NAICS code is structurally outside the roll-up lens. Census defines it as government establishments, the dataset supplies neither a receipts-based labor share nor an LMM firm count, and there is no privately transferable operator population.
narrative.ai_impact: Language and analytic systems can draft and summarize materials, classify submissions, check completeness, answer routine questions, extract evidence, reconcile reports, and prioritize reviews. Accountable officials must still interpret law and policy, allocate funds, supervise programs, resolve exceptions, protect sensitive data, and make consequential decisions.
narrative.value_capture: Operational benefits can improve service, reduce backlogs, or free public resources, but they do not become commercial margin retained by an in-industry owner. Appropriations, oversight, procurement, and public-budget processes determine where savings go.
narrative.firm_availability: There are no eligible firms under the published industry definition. Private software, consulting, data, and administrative-service vendors may be acquisition targets in their own NAICS industries, but importing them here would violate the frozen population.
narrative.demand_durability: Education administration remains necessary despite a modest projected enrollment decline because grants, policy, intergovernmental coordination, compliance, statistics, and public accountability persist. The volume and organizational location of the work remain exposed to legislative change, program consolidation, and shifts between federal and state authority.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Task exposure is inferred from functions rather than measured time use, and implementation evidence is broader federal-government evidence. Privacy, security, legacy systems, procurement, records law, accessibility, and rights-impacting decisions can slow adoption. The decisive issue is not technology uncertainty but the absence of a transferable commercial firm population.
narrative.principal_driver: Information-intensive grant, policy, reporting, and data workflows create substantial AI-assistance potential inside public agencies.
narrative.principal_weakness: The industry is government-only, so no privately owned LMM operator can satisfy the fixed acquisition lens.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 923110 Administration of Education Programs
sources.supports: Defines the industry as government establishments centrally coordinating, planning, supervising, and administering education funds, policies, intergovernmental activities, statistical reports, data collection, and programs.

sources.title: Office of State and Grantee Support Services
sources.supports: Examples of education-program administration work: allocating funds, technical assistance, fiduciary accountability, compliance monitoring, and data collection and analysis.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence: Generative AI Use and Management at Federal Agencies
sources.supports: Potential for generative AI in internal operations and service delivery, ninefold growth in agency generative-AI use from 2023 to 2024, and reported policy, privacy, and technology-management obstacles.

sources.title: OMB Memorandum M-25-21: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust
sources.supports: Federal AI adoption, governance, public-trust, accountable-official, and high-impact-use risk-management requirements.

sources.title: Projections of Education Statistics: Elementary and Secondary Enrollment
sources.supports: Projects public and private elementary and secondary enrollment to decline from 2020 to 2030 and describes the projection method and regional pattern.

sources.title: Grantees and Applicants
sources.supports: Ongoing administration of formula and discretionary grants to state and local education agencies and other education stakeholders.
